heb.paragraph: The noun mimētēs (v. 1) calls believers to be ‘imitators of God’ (mimētai tou theou)—an audacious command grounded in the believers’ status as ‘dearly loved children.’ The imitatio Dei theme is filtered through Christ’s self-giving love (v. 2): imitating God means walking in love as Christ walked.

The light-darkness contrast (vv. 8–14) is both ontological and ethical. Believers are not merely told to behave as light but are declared to be light: ‘you are light in the Lord’ (v. 8). The ethical imperative follows from the ontological identity: ‘live as children of light.’ The fruit of the light consists in ‘all goodness, righteousness and truth’ (v. 9).

refs.note: John’s declaration that ‘God is light; in him there is no darkness at all’ provides the theological basis for the light-darkness ethic of Eph 5:8–14.
Jesus’ declaration ‘you are the light of the world’ parallels Paul’s affirmation ‘you are light in the Lord’ (Eph 5:8), with both passages deriving ethical imperatives from ontological identity.

notes.note: Imitating God is not abstract admiration but concrete behaviour: walking in love as Christ loved. MacArthur notes the contrast: the self-giving love of Christ (v. 2) versus the self-indulgent vices of vv. 3–4. Sexual immorality and greed are singled out as incompatible with the community of saints.

The warning that the sexually immoral and greedy have no inheritance in the kingdom is not hypothetical but absolute. MacArthur reads this as describing the habitual lifestyle of the unregenerate, not isolated lapses by believers: a life characterised by these practices evidences the absence of regeneration.

The ontological statement ‘you are light in the Lord’ grounds the ethical call. Believers do not merely carry light; they are light. The practical implications: expose darkness (v. 11), avoid secret shameful acts (v. 12), and live transparently. The hymn fragment of v. 14 functions as a call to spiritual awakening.

Calvin reads the imitatio Dei in terms of the believer’s adoption: children naturally resemble their father. Since God is love, his children walk in love. The pattern is Christ’s self-offering—a ‘fragrant offering and sacrifice’ (v. 2)—echoing the OT language of acceptable sacrifice.

Calvin emphasises the ontological transformation: ‘you were once darkness, but now you are light.’ The language is not of moral improvement but of fundamental change. Darkness is not merely ignorance but the state of being alienated from God; light is not merely knowledge but participation in God’s own nature.

The phrase prosphoran kai thysian (offering and sacrifice) uses two near-synonyms for emphasis. The NET notes the OT sacrificial background and the additional phrase ‘a fragrant aroma’ (osmēn euōdias), echoing Gen 8:21 and Exod 29:18.

The quotation formula dio legei (therefore it says) introduces a text that does not match any known OT passage exactly. The NET translators suggest it may be a free adaptation of Isa 26:19 and 60:1, or an early Christian hymn.

Lincoln reads the section as structured by two walking metaphors: walk in love (vv. 1–7) and walk as children of light (vv. 8–14). The vice catalogue (vv. 3–5) and the warning against deception (v. 6) address the tension between the Ephesian Christians and their pagan environment. The hymn fragment (v. 14) may reflect baptismal liturgy, in which the convert rises from the ‘death’ of sin to the ‘light’ of Christ.

O’Brien identifies the passage’s organising principle as the imitatio Dei: the command to imitate God (v. 1) governs both the love section (vv. 1–7) and the light section (vv. 8–14). God’s character as love and light defines the ethical standard. O’Brien notes the shift from negative (avoiding vice) to positive (producing the fruit of light), reflecting the put-off/put-on pattern of 4:22–24.

hist.context: Paul continues the ethical instruction of ch. 4, organising it around two metaphors: love (vv. 1–7) and light (vv. 8–14). The call to imitate God by walking in love (vv. 1–2) is followed by a catalogue of vices incompatible with the kingdom: sexual immorality, impurity, greed, obscenity, foolish talk, and coarse joking (vv. 3–4). Paul warns that no immoral, impure, or greedy person has any inheritance in the kingdom (v. 5). The light section (vv. 8–14) contrasts the believers’ former darkness with their present status as ‘light in the Lord.’ The quoted fragment in v. 14—‘Wake up, sleeper, rise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you’—may be an early Christian hymn or baptismal formula, echoing Isaiah 60:1.

heb.paragraph: The command plērousthe en pneumati (v. 18) is the central imperative of the passage. The present passive imperative indicates a continuous, repeated experience, not a once-for-all event. The four participles that follow (speaking, singing, giving thanks, submitting) describe the lifestyle that characterises Spirit-filled believers. The contrast with drunkenness (v. 18a) is not merely moral but functional: just as wine controls and influences the drunkard, the Spirit is to control and influence the believer.

refs.note: The parallel passage in Colossians uses nearly identical language but substitutes ‘the word of Christ’ for ‘the Spirit’ as the controlling influence: ‘Let the message of Christ dwell among you richly.’ The parallels suggest that being filled with the Spirit and being controlled by Christ’s word are complementary, not competing, descriptions.

The Pentecost narrative provides the background for the wine/Spirit contrast: the disciples were accused of being drunk (Acts 2:13), but Peter explained that they were filled with the Spirit (Acts 2:4, 15–16).

notes.note: Wise living is defined by discerning and obeying God’s will. MacArthur notes that ‘redeeming the time’ (KJV) or ‘making the most of every opportunity’ reflects the urgency of life in a fallen world: opportunities for kingdom service are limited and must be seized.

The Spirit-filling command is the practical key to the Christian life. MacArthur distinguishes between the indwelling of the Spirit (which is permanent for all believers) and the filling of the Spirit (which is a repeated experience of the Spirit’s control). The four participles describe the community’s worship life: Spirit-filled believers address one another in song, give thanks in all circumstances, and submit to one another.

Calvin reads the wine-Spirit contrast as a warning against seeking emotional or spiritual experience through artificial means. Drunkenness leads to debauchery (asotia); the Spirit leads to wisdom, gratitude, and mutual submission. Calvin insists that being filled with the Spirit is not an ecstatic experience but a sober, continuous state of yielded obedience.

Calvin reads mutual submission as the foundational principle of the household code. Submission is not one-directional but reciprocal: it applies to all members of the community in relation to one another, though it takes different forms in different relationships.

The present passive imperative plērousthe commands continuous action. The preposition en may be instrumental (‘by the Spirit’) or locative (‘with the Spirit’ as content). The NET translators favour the instrumental reading: the Spirit is the agent of filling.

The three terms—psalmois, hymnois, odais pneumatikais—may denote OT psalms, Christian hymns, and Spirit-inspired songs respectively, though the distinctions are debated. The phrase en tē kardia hymōn (in your hearts) locates the music’s source in genuine inner devotion.

Lincoln identifies v. 18 as the passage’s central command, with the four participles functioning as its outworking. The mutual submission of v. 21 is the hinge between the general paraenesis (4:1–5:21) and the household code (5:22–6:9). Lincoln argues that v. 21’s reciprocal submission qualifies and shapes all that follows.

O’Brien reads the passage as a progression: wisdom (vv. 15–17) is the mindset, Spirit-filling (v. 18) is the means, and the four participial expressions (vv. 19–21) are the outworking. O’Brien argues that mutual submission (v. 21) is not a separate ethical principle but a dimension of being Spirit-filled: the Spirit’s control produces a community characterised by voluntary, Christ-motivated deference.

hist.context: Paul moves from the light metaphor to the wisdom theme: ‘Be very careful, then, how you live—not as unwise but as wise’ (v. 15). Wisdom is exercised by ‘making the most of every opportunity, because the days are evil’ (v. 16) and by understanding the Lord’s will (v. 17). The antithesis of wine-intoxication and Spirit-filling (v. 18) introduces four expressions of the Spirit-filled life: mutual address through psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs (v. 19a); singing and making music in the heart (v. 19b); giving thanks always for everything (v. 20); and mutual submission out of reverence for Christ (v. 21). This final participle—‘submitting to one another’—serves as the transition to the household code that follows (5:22–6:9).

sections.header: Verses 22–33 — "Christ and the Church: A Profound Mystery"

heb.paragraph: The reappearance of mystērion in v. 32 applies the term to the one-flesh union of husband and wife as a reflection of the Christ-church relationship. Paul declares: ‘This is a profound mystery—but I am talking about Christ and the church.’ The marriage relationship is not merely illustrated by the Christ-church analogy; it is grounded in it. The earthly institution reflects the eternal reality.

refs.note: The parallel household code in Colossians provides the abbreviated version: wives submit, husbands love. Ephesians develops the theological rationale at much greater length.
The creation ordinance of one-flesh union is quoted in Eph 5:31 and interpreted typologically: the original marriage institution points beyond itself to the ultimate union of Christ and the church.
The eschatological marriage supper of the Lamb provides the apocalyptic fulfilment of the Christ-church marriage metaphor developed in Eph 5:25–32.

notes.note: The wife’s submission is voluntary and relational, not compelled or absolute. It is ‘as to the Lord’—motivated by devotion to Christ, not fear of the husband. MacArthur notes that submission is not inferiority: the church is not inferior to Christ, yet the church submits to Christ’s headship.

The husband’s love is patterned on Christ’s self-sacrifice: ‘gave himself up for her.’ This is not sentimental affection but self-denying, sacrificial action aimed at the spouse’s sanctification. MacArthur notes that Christ’s love for the church sets an infinitely high standard: the husband is to love with the same quality (though not degree) of self-giving love.

The Gen 2:24 quotation and its application to Christ and the church reveal that marriage was designed from creation to be a picture of the gospel. MacArthur insists that this is the highest view of marriage in all of Scripture: it is not merely a human institution but a reflection of the cosmic love story between Christ and his people.

Calvin reads mutual submission (v. 21) as the general principle, with the husband-wife instructions as its primary application. The wife’s submission and the husband’s love are not competing demands but complementary expressions of the Christ-church relationship. Calvin insists that the husband’s authority is not license for tyranny but a call to servant-leadership patterned on Christ.

Calvin interprets the ‘profound mystery’ as the gospel itself: the one-flesh union of marriage was always intended to prefigure Christ’s union with his church. Calvin notes that this is not allegory but typology: the historical institution of marriage bears a divinely intended pattern that finds its fulfilment in Christ and the church.

The verb ‘submit’ (hypotassō) does not appear in v. 22 in the earliest manuscripts; it is supplied from v. 21. The NET translators note this indicates that v. 22 is syntactically dependent on v. 21: the wife’s submission is a specific instance of the mutual submission commanded in v. 21.

The phrase to mystērion touto mega estin (this mystery is great) uses mega as a predicate adjective: the mystery is ‘profound’ or ‘great’ in significance. The clarification ego de legō eis Christon kai eis tēn ekklēsian (I am speaking with reference to Christ and the church) prevents misreading: the primary referent is not marriage in itself but the Christ-church relationship.

Lincoln reads the household code against the background of Greco-Roman household management literature (Aristotle, Pol. 1253b; Philo, Hypothetica). Paul transforms the conventional pattern by christologising each role: the wife’s submission is ‘as to the Lord,’ the husband’s love is ‘as Christ loved the church.’ Lincoln argues that the theological weight falls overwhelmingly on the husband’s obligation to self-sacrificial love, which is developed at three times the length of the wife’s instruction.

O’Brien notes that the Christ-church analogy is not merely illustrative but constitutive: marriage reflects and participates in the Christ-church relationship. O’Brien reads the passage in light of the letter’s overarching theme of unity: the husband-wife relationship is one dimension of the cosmic unity God is creating in Christ. The household code is not a concession to cultural convention but a theological claim: the structures of daily life are the arena where the mystery of Christ and the church is lived out.

hist.context: The household code (Haustafel) begins with the husband-wife relationship (5:22–33). Paul addresses wives (vv. 22–24) and husbands (vv. 25–33) within the framework of the Christ-church relationship. The wife’s submission to her husband is ‘as to the Lord’ (v. 22), grounded in the analogy of the church’s submission to Christ as head (v. 23). The husband’s love for his wife is ‘just as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her’ (v. 25)—a standard of sacrificial, self-giving love far exceeding mere authority. The passage climaxes with the quotation of Gen 2:24 (‘the two will become one flesh’) and Paul’s identification of this mystery with Christ and the church (vv. 31–32). The theological weight falls on the Christ-church relationship, which both models and empowers the marriage relationship.

themes.note: Holiness dominates through the extensive ethical instruction. Worship is high through the Spirit-filled worship life (vv. 19–20). Covenant is prominent through the marriage-as-mystery theme, which grounds human covenant relationships in the Christ-church covenant.

rows.label: vv.1–2
rows.text: Imitators of God: walk in love as Christ loved

rows.label: vv.3–7
rows.text: Vice catalogue: sexual immorality, greed, coarse talk excluded

rows.label: vv.8–14
rows.text: Light and darkness: you are light; expose deeds of darkness

rows.label: vv.15–17
rows.text: Walk wisely: redeem the time in evil days

rows.label: v.18
rows.text: Central command: be filled with the Spirit, not wine

rows.label: vv.19–21
rows.text: Spirit-filled life: psalms, thanksgiving, mutual submission

rows.label: vv.22–24
rows.text: Wives: submit as the church submits to Christ

rows.label: vv.25–33
rows.text: Husbands: love as Christ loved the church; the profound mystery

lit.note: Ephesians 5 transitions from the light-darkness ethic (vv. 1–14) through the Spirit-filling imperative (vv. 15–21) to the household code’s marriage instruction (vv. 22–33). The chapter’s central command is ‘be filled with the Spirit’ (v. 18), and its theological climax is the identification of marriage as a mystery reflecting Christ and the church (v. 32).

hebtext.note: The noun mimētēs (v. 1) calls believers to be ‘imitators of God’ (mimētai tou theou)—an audacious command grounded in the believers’ status as ‘dearly loved children.’ The imitatio Dei theme is filtered through Christ’s self-giving love (v. 2): imitating God means walking in love as Christ walked.

The light-darkness contrast (vv. 8–14) is both ontological and ethical. Believers are not merely told to behave as light but are declared to be light: ‘you are light in the Lord’ (v. 8). The ethical imperative follows from the ontological identity: ‘live as children of light.’ The fruit of the light consists in ‘all goodness, righteousness and truth’ (v. 9).

The command plērousthe en pneumati (v. 18) is the central imperative of the passage. The present passive imperative indicates a continuous, repeated experience, not a once-for-all event. The four participles that follow (speaking, singing, giving thanks, submitting) describe the lifestyle that characterises Spirit-filled believers. The contrast with drunkenness (v. 18a) is not merely moral but functional: just as wine controls and influences the drunkard, the Spirit is to control and influence the believer.

The reappearance of mystērion in v. 32 applies the term to the one-flesh union of husband and wife as a reflection of the Christ-church relationship. Paul declares: ‘This is a profound mystery—but I am talking about Christ and the church.’ The marriage relationship is not merely illustrated by the Christ-church analogy; it is grounded in it. The earthly institution reflects the eternal reality.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: 14—‘Wake up, sleeper, rise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you’—may be an early Christian hymn or baptismal formula, echoing Isaiah 60:1…

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Walking in Love and Light within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: John’s declaration that ‘God is light; in him there is no darkness at all’ provides the theological basis for the light-darkness ethic of Eph 5:8–14.
Jesus’ declaration ‘you are the light of the world’ parallels Paul’s affirmation ‘you are light in the Lord’ (Eph 5:8), with both passages deriving ethical imperatives from ontological identity.

The parallel passage in Colossians uses nearly identical language but substitutes ‘the word of Christ’ for ‘the Spirit’ as the controlling influence: ‘Let the message of Christ dwell among you richly.’ The parallels suggest that being filled with the…

The Pentecost narrative provides the background for the wine/Spirit contrast: the disciples were accused of being drunk (Acts 2:13), but Peter explained that they were filled with the Spirit (Acts 2:4, 15–16).
The parallel household code in Colossians provides the abbreviated version: wives submit, husbands love. Ephesians develops the theological rationale at much greater length.
The creation ordinance of one-flesh union is quoted in Eph 5:31 and interpreted typologically: the original marriage institution points beyond itself to the ultimate union of Christ and the church.

discourse.thesis: Imitate God as beloved children — walking in love, light, and wisdom — and submit to one another in the fear of Christ, as modeled in the marriage relationship that mirrors Christ and the church.

nodes.text: Imitate (μιμηταί) God as beloved children and walk in love (ἐν ἀγάπῃ) as Christ loved us and gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God. The model for Christian ethics is theomorphic — shaped by the character of God and the self-giving love of Christ.

Sexual immorality, impurity, covetousness must not even be named among you — as befits saints. No filthiness or foolish talk. Warnings about inheriting the kingdom. Walk as children of light (τέκνα φωτός) — the fruit of light is goodness, righteousness, and truth. Expose the fruitless works of darkness — light makes everything visible.

children.text: Identity grounds ethics: you were darkness (σκότος), but now you are light (φῶς) in the Lord — therefore walk as children of light. The imperative is rooted in the indicative: be what you are. The ethical demand flows from ontological transformation.

When anything is exposed by the light, it becomes visible — everything exposed is light. The early Christian hymn (v. 14): Awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you. Possibly a baptismal hymn, calling the newly baptized out of spiritual death into Christ's light.

nodes.text: Walk wisely, not unwisely — making the best use of the time (ἐξαγοραζόμενοι τὸν καιρόν) because the days are evil. Understand the Lord's will. Be filled with the Spirit (πληροῦσθε ἐν πνεύματι) — present imperative, continuous action — as expressed in community: singing psalms and hymns, giving thanks, submitting to one another out of reverence for Christ.

children.text: Do not get drunk with wine (in which is debauchery) but be filled with the Spirit. The contrast is striking: both drunkenness and Spirit-filling alter behavior and community character. The grammar is crucial — 'be filled' is passive (receive filling) and plural (a community action), not solo performance.

The five participles (singing, making melody, giving thanks, submitting) describe the Spirit-filled community life. Mutual submission (v. 21) is the hinge connecting Spirit-filling to the household code — it is not merely an add-on but the governing principle of all that follows.

nodes.text: The household code — wives to husbands as to the Lord (v. 22), husbands love wives as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her (v. 25). The marriage analogy: the husband is head (κεφαλή) as Christ is head of the church his body. Christ's love for the church is sacrificial, sanctifying, and presenting — the model for the husband's love.

children.text: Christ's purpose for the church: to sanctify her, having cleansed her by the washing of water with the word, so that he might present the church to himself in splendor — no spot or wrinkle or blemish, holy and blameless. This is eschatological vision: the church's final glorification is the goal of Christ's present, sacrificial love.

Genesis 2:24 ('the two shall become one flesh') is cited as the great mystery (μυστήριον μέγα) — which Paul refers to Christ and the church. Human marriage is not merely an analogy for Christ/church — it is its sacramental embodiment. The practical summary: husband love your wife; wife respect your husband.

discourse.note: Ephesians 5 weaves together imitation ethics (vv. 1-2), purity ethics (vv. 3-14), wisdom ethics (vv. 15-21), and relational ethics (vv. 22-33). The household code is not a concession to Roman social order but a transformation of it: the husband's love is defined by Christ's self-giving, not Roman paterfamilias authority. The mutual submission of v. 21 governs the entire code.
